meta.title: 1.4 User Segments and Their Failure Modes
meta.description: The hardest product design challenge in platform work is serving multiple user segments simultaneously without degrading the experience for any of them. Make's three segments have incompatible mental

blocks.prompt:
> Figma Make serves three user types: developers, non-technical builders, and AI agents. How does each group break the platform differently?

---

blocks.content:
The hardest product design challenge in platform work is serving multiple user segments simultaneously without degrading the experience for any of them. Make's three segments have incompatible mental models, incompatible failure modes, and incompatible recovery strategies. The platform must serve all three — or explicitly choose which to prioritize and accept the consequences.

**Segment 1: Developers**

Developers using Make are experienced engineers who understand the code being generated. They use Make for speed — to accelerate scaffolding, to prototype ideas, to generate boilerplate — not because they cannot write the code themselves.

**What they want:**
- Framework opinions they can live with (or override)
- Code they can read, understand, and modify without fighting the generator
- Escape hatches: the ability to drop down to raw code when the abstraction fails
- Integration with their existing toolchain: Git, CI/CD, their preferred IDE
- Deployment targets they control (AWS, GCP, their own Kubernetes cluster)

**How they break the platform:**
Developers exceed the abstraction. They want to add a custom webpack plugin, configure ESLint rules, use a specific version of a dependency, or restructure the project in a way Make's generator doesn't expect. When they do this, the generated code and the manual modifications conflict. The platform's model of the project diverges from the actual project state.

This is the "synchronization problem" — Make generates code based on a model of the project; the developer modifies the project outside that model. On the next generation, Make may overwrite the developer's changes, or generate code that conflicts with the modified state.

The recovery path for developers: they can read the code, understand the conflict, and resolve it manually. The cost is friction and lost time.

**The abstraction gradient for developers:** They want visible code, editable configuration, CLI access, and integration depth. The platform must expose these without making them required for simpler users.

**Segment 2: Non-Technical Builders**

Non-technical builders are designers, PMs, marketers, and domain experts who want software without the software engineering. They are not learning to code — they are trying to build a product. The code is a means to an end, not a thing of value in itself.

**What they want:**
- Natural language interaction throughout (not just at prompt time)
- Visual verification: I can see it works without reading code
- Automatic error recovery: if something breaks, fix it without asking me to understand why
- Simple deployment: one click, done, it's live
- No maintenance burden: the platform keeps it running

**How they break the platform:**
Non-technical builders hit errors they cannot interpret. "Module not found: Can't resolve '@/components/Button'" is meaningless to someone who has never seen a module system. The error message is developer-oriented; the user is not a developer. They cannot debug, cannot read the code, cannot tell the agent what specifically went wrong.

The failure mode is abandonment. Non-technical users who hit an unrecoverable error do not file bug reports — they leave. The platform's quality bar must be much higher for this segment because the recovery path is much narrower.

A secondary failure mode: non-technical users generate apps that work but are not secure. They deploy without reviewing for hardcoded secrets. They ship apps with no error handling because the demo worked. The platform has a safety responsibility to this segment that it does not have for developers (who can evaluate risk themselves).

**The abstraction gradient for non-technical builders:** They want no code visible, natural language everything, automatic error handling, and guardrails. The platform must hide complexity without pretending it doesn't exist.

**Segment 3: AI Agents**

This is the emerging segment — other AI systems using Make's platform as infrastructure. Make's Canvas-Direct AI Agents capability (April 2026) means autonomous agents can generate, modify, and deploy applications without human prompting per step. The agent is the user.

**What they need:**
- Stable, programmatic APIs (not a UI)
- Deterministic error responses (machine-readable, not human-readable)
- Rate limits that accommodate agent request patterns (bursty, high-volume)
- Execution sandboxes that tolerate unusual code patterns
- Webhooks and callbacks for async results (agents don't wait at a UI)

**How they break the platform:**
Agents generate edge-case code at scale. An agent exploring the solution space may generate 50 variations of a component, each subtly different, to find the optimal one. It may call Make's APIs in patterns that look like abuse from a rate-limiting perspective. It may generate code that is syntactically valid but semantically unusual — patterns the preview infrastructure has never seen.

Agents also fail at the tail of the distribution. A human user prompts Make 5–10 times per session. An agent may prompt 500 times. The 1% of prompts that produce broken previews become 5 prompts for a human user (annoying but tolerable) and 5 failures per agent session (a systematic problem).

**The abstraction gradient for agents:** They want API-first access, machine-readable responses, high rate limits, and programmatic deployment. They do not want a UI.

**Why Serving All Three Simultaneously is the Hardest Problem**

The abstraction gradients for each segment point in different directions:
- Developers want less abstraction (more control)
- Non-technical builders want more abstraction (less complexity)
- Agents want no UI at all (just APIs)

A single UI cannot satisfy all three. The platform must architect multiple surfaces:
- A visual canvas for non-technical builders (maximum abstraction)
- A code view and CLI for developers (minimum abstraction)
- A REST/GraphQL API for agents (no UI)

And these surfaces must share the same underlying runtime, the same preview infrastructure, and the same deployment pipeline. The complexity of this multi-surface architecture is why Make's Platform PM role is not a feature PM role — it is an infrastructure PM role.

**The Figma Analogy**

Figma itself has this three-segment problem:
- Professional designers want every tool, every plugin, every precision control
- Non-designers want templates and AI magic that produces good output without training
- Developers want accurate design tokens, Dev Mode, and API access

Figma's answer: layers of abstraction with escape hatches. Non-designers use templates and AI; professionals use components and auto-layout; developers use Dev Mode and the REST API. The same underlying system, multiple surfaces.

Make must build the same layered abstraction — and the Platform PM must design the layers before the engineering team implements them.

---

blocks.question: A non-technical user's deployed Make app is returning 500 errors in production because an environment variable was not set on the deployment platform. The user cannot read the logs, cannot identify the missing variable, and cannot fix it without help. Walk through how the platform should handle this — at each layer.
blocks.answer:
Layer 1 — Prevention: The platform should detect required environment variables at build/deploy time and prompt the user to set them before deployment completes. Layer 2 — Detection: The platform should monitor deployed apps for error rates and alert the user in natural language ("Your app is returning errors — here's what we think is wrong"). Layer 3 — Recovery: The platform should offer a one-click "diagnose and fix" flow that identifies the missing variable, prompts the user to supply a value, and redeploys. Developers would find the logs and fix it manually. Non-technical users need all three layers to have any recovery path.*

---

blocks.title:
Take a Make (or Lovable/Bolt) generated app and deliberately introduce a runtime error — remove a required prop, delete an import, introduce a typo in an API URL. Then observe what the platform shows the user. Is the error message interpretable by a non-technical user? What recovery options does the platform offer? Grade the error experience for each of the three segments.

---

blocks.prompt:
Explain to a Figma VP of Product why the decision to "serve developers and non-technical builders" is actually a more complex platform architecture decision than it sounds — using the concept of abstraction gradients.

---

questions: > In production AI platforms serving multiple internal user segments — ML engineers, product teams, operations teams — how does the abstraction gradient problem manifest?

How have successful teams handled this tradeoff, and what would carry over to Make's segment architecture?

---

blocks.content:
Developers, non-technical builders, and AI agents each break Make's platform in structurally different ways — requiring not one product surface but three abstraction layers (canvas, code view, API) sharing a single underlying runtime, which is the defining architectural challenge of the Platform PM role.

---

blocks.prompt:
> Every segment's needs connect back to one question: what does Figma uniquely have that makes Make worth building here rather than as a standalone tool? That's Make's competitive position — and it's next.

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: Developers using Make hit what the module calls the 'synchronization problem.' What exactly causes this, and why is it structurally different from the failures non-technical builders face?
options: Developers cause synchronization problems by using Make on slow internet connections, which causes the preview to lag behind their edits
Synchronization problems occur when two developers use Make on the same project simultaneously, causing merge conflicts

Developers modify the project outside Make's model of the project state; on the next generation, Make may overwrite their changes or generate code that conflicts with the manual modifications — a failure only possible for users who can access and edit the underlying code
reviewQuestions.explanation: The synchronization problem is specific to developers precisely because they have the ability to modify generated code directly. When a developer restructures files, changes configuration, or edits components manually, Make's internal model of the project diverges from the actual state. The next generation may overwrite those changes. Non-technical users can't edit the underlying code, so they can't cause this failure mode — but they also can't recover from errors that require code-level intervention.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly — the synchronization problem is a failure mode exclusive to users with code access, which is why segment-specific failure analysis matters for platform design.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Network speed and multi-user editing are different problems. The synchronization problem is specifically about Make's internal model diverging from the actual project state when a developer makes manual code changes. It's a failure mode that only affects users who can edit the code — non-technical builders can't cause it, but also can't fix errors that require code-level intervention.

reviewQuestions.question: Why do AI agents (as a third user segment) require different infrastructure design than either developers or non-technical builders?
options: Agents interact programmatically via APIs, generate requests in bursty high-volume patterns, need machine-readable error responses, and amplify platform reliability problems since a 1% failure rate for human users becomes systematic failures at agent scale
Agents require a visual UI with better accessibility support since they interact with Make's canvas rather than through text prompts
Agents are simply faster developers — they need the same infrastructure as developers, just with higher rate limits
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module's key insight about agents is that they amplify platform weaknesses. A human makes 5-10 prompts per session; an agent may make 500. A 1% failure rate that produces 5 broken previews for a human (annoying, tolerable) produces 5 systematic failures per agent session (a reliability crisis). Agents also need API-first access with machine-readable errors — they cannot interpret developer-oriented error messages or navigate a UI. They're not faster developers; they're a different interface paradigm entirely.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — the amplification dynamic is the most important insight about agents as a segment, and it changes how Make must think about reliability targets.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Agents don't use a visual UI — they need API access. And they're not just faster developers: the rate at which they issue requests and their inability to interpret human-readable errors make them a categorically different interface paradigm. The amplification effect on reliability is the critical infrastructure implication the module highlights.

reviewQuestions.question: The module proposes that Make needs three distinct product surfaces — a visual canvas, a code view/CLI, and a REST/GraphQL API — sharing a single underlying runtime. Why must they share a runtime, and what makes this architecturally challenging?
options: They must share a runtime for cost reasons — maintaining three separate runtimes would triple infrastructure costs

All three surfaces must produce identical behavior from the same generated code, so the execution environment must be consistent — but each surface requires opposite abstraction levels (maximum, minimum, and none), which requires building multiple interface layers on top of a single consistent execution core
Sharing a runtime is a regulatory requirement since all user data must be processed in the same environment for compliance
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module frames this as the defining architectural challenge: the three segments need opposite abstraction levels (canvas hides everything; code view exposes everything; API eliminates the UI). But if each surface ran against a different runtime, the same app could behave differently depending on which surface generated it — a correctness problem. The platform must abstract the interface layer while keeping the execution layer unified. This is harder than it sounds: the developer's code edits, the non-technical builder's natural language edits, and the agent's API calls must all converge on the same project state.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Good — the 'same underlying runtime with multiple surfaces' constraint is exactly what makes this an infrastructure PM challenge rather than a feature PM challenge.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Cost and compliance are secondary concerns here. The core reason for shared runtime is behavioral consistency: if the canvas, code view, and API ran on different execution environments, the same app might behave differently depending on which surface was used to generate it. Shared runtime ensures consistent output; multiple surfaces satisfy the incompatible abstraction requirements of each segment.
